1:
"In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth." The witnesses note the creation of the universe by God in the beginning. On "In the beginning God created," the grammatical voices note the absolute commencement. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "in the beginning (bereshith) God created (bara) the heaven and the earth" marks the absolute beginning of the created order; "in the beginning" is the commencement of time and of all things; before it, only the eternal God. The verb "created" (bara), used of God's distinctive making, denotes the bringing into being of what did not exist; the idea of creation out of nothing (ex nihilo) is contained in the form and use of the word — God produces the universe by his word and will, not from pre-existing matter.

On "God created," the reformed Calvin and Gill note creation ex nihilo. Calvin and Gill note that God, the eternal Creator, brought the heaven and earth into being out of nothing; the noble idea of producing something from nothing (Clarke) marks the divine, omnipotent act; "Elohim," the plural-form name of God, is joined to the singular verb, the eternal God the sole Creator.

On "the heaven and the earth," the eastern Basil the Great and the grammatical-historical note the totality: "the heaven and the earth" is the comprehensive expression for the whole created universe (the totality of all things); Basil, in the Hexaemeron, reads the verse as the foundation of all — the visible universe created by God in the beginning, the work then unfolded in the six days. The verse displays the creation of the universe by God in the beginning.

The witnesses agree the substance: in the beginning God created the heaven and the earth — the absolute commencement of all things, the universe brought into being out of nothing by the eternal God (Keil, Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Basil). The verse marks the beginning: of all things. The verse displays the Creator: God. The verse displays the creation: ex nihilo. The verse displays the universe: heaven and earth. The act displays God making the world out of nothing by his word. The verse displays the beginning: absolute. The verse displays the created: heaven and earth. The verse displays the God: eternal Creator. The creation of the universe by God in the beginning displays the foundation of all Scripture: in the beginning God created the heaven and the earth — the eternal God bringing the whole universe into being out of nothing, the commencement of time and the work unfolded in the days that follow. The verse displays the created world, at first unformed and void (v.2).
2:
"And the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters." The witnesses note the unformed earth and the Spirit moving on the waters. On "the earth was without form, and void," the grammatical voices note the initial state. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the earth was without form, and void (tohu va-bohu)" — the earth, newly created, was at first an unformed, empty, chaotic mass; not yet shaped, ordered, or filled; the raw material out of which God would, in the six days, form and furnish the world; "darkness was upon the face of the deep (tehom)" — darkness covered the watery abyss.

On "darkness was upon the face of the deep," the reformed Calvin notes the chaos. Calvin and Gill note that the deep (the primeval waters) was covered with darkness; the earth lay in a confused, dark, watery state, awaiting God's forming word; the chaos is not eternal but a stage in God's creative work.

On "the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters," interpreters note the Spirit. The reformed Calvin and Gill and the eastern Basil take "the Spirit of God" as the Holy Spirit, who brooded or hovered over the waters (like a bird over its nest), cherishing and quickening the unformed mass, sustaining and preparing it for the order to come; the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch likewise read the Spirit of God as the divine creative power, brooding to quicken and order the chaos (some render "a wind of God," but the personal Spirit is the fuller reading). The verse displays the unformed earth and the Spirit moving on the waters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the initial state — "the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep," the newly created earth an unformed, dark, watery chaos awaiting God's forming (Keil, Ellicott, Calvin); "the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters," the Holy Spirit brooding over and quickening the unformed mass (Calvin, Gill, Basil, Keil). The verse displays the brooding Spirit. The verse marks the earth: unformed. The verse displays the deep: dark. The verse displays the Spirit: moving. The verse displays the waters: brooded over. The vision displays the chaos quickened by the Spirit. The verse displays the earth: void. The verse displays the Spirit: of God. The verse displays the brooding: cherishing. The unformed earth and the Spirit moving on the waters display the second stage of creation: the earth was without form and void, darkness upon the deep, and the Spirit of God brooded over the waters — the unformed, dark chaos quickened and prepared by the Spirit for the order to come. The verse displays the brooded chaos, into which God speaks light (v.3).
3:
"And God said, Let there be light: and there was light." The witnesses note the creation of light by God's word. On "God said, Let there be light," the grammatical voices note the creative word. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God said, Let there be light" — God creates by his word; the first creative fiat (Psalm 33:6, 9, "he spake, and it was done"); light is called into being by the divine command; the word of God is the efficient cause of creation, and the New Testament identifies this Word as the eternal Son (John 1:1-3).

On "Let there be light," the reformed Calvin and Gill note the first work of the days. Calvin and Gill note that light is the first thing created in the ordering of the world; before the sun (made on the fourth day), God brings forth light; the light, however produced, dispels the primeval darkness; the beginning of order and beauty in the creation.

On "and there was light," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the instant effect: "and there was light" — the word was instantly effective; light came at once at God's command; the immediacy and efficacy of the divine word (Henry: God said it, and it was so); the contrast of the word spoken and the thing done marks the omnipotence of God's creative speech. The verse displays the creation of light by God's word.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creative word — "God said, Let there be light: and there was light," God calling light into being by his word, the first creative fiat (Psalm 33:6; John 1:3, Keil, Ellicott); the light the first work, dispelling the darkness before the sun (Calvin, Gill); "and there was light," the instant, efficacious effect of the divine word (Henry). The verse displays the created light. The verse marks the word: let there be light. The verse displays the command: God's. The verse displays the effect: there was light. The verse displays the efficacy: instant. The act displays light called into being by God's word. The verse displays the word: spoken. The verse displays the light: created. The verse displays the darkness: dispelled. The creation of light by God's word displays the first work of the days: God said, "Let there be light," and there was light — light called into being by the omnipotent word of God, the first ordering of the creation. The verse displays the created light, seen to be good (v.4).
4:
"And God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the light from the darkness." The brief verse notes God seeing the light good and dividing it from darkness. The grammatical voices note the divine approval. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God saw the light, that it was good" — God surveys his work and pronounces it good; the refrain "it was good" (recurring through the chapter) marks the perfection, fitness, and beauty of each work; the creation answers God's design and is approved by him.

On "God saw the light, that it was good," the reformed Calvin and Gill note the approbation. Calvin and Gill note that God's seeing the light good is his approval of it — not that he learned anything, but that he pronounces and establishes its goodness; the light is fit for its purpose; the divine approbation seals each work as good.

On "God divided the light from the darkness," the grammatical-historical note the separation: "God divided (separated) the light from the darkness" — God orders the light and darkness into a distinction, assigning each its place and time (day and night, v.5); the first act of separating and ordering the creation; light and darkness are distinguished by God's appointment. The verse displays God seeing the light good and dividing it from darkness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the approval — "God saw the light, that it was good," God surveying and approving his work, the refrain of goodness (Keil, Ellicott); God's seeing good his approbation, sealing the work (Calvin, Gill); "God divided the light from the darkness," the ordering of light and darkness into distinction (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the good light. The verse marks the seeing: it was good. The verse displays the approval: God's. The verse displays the dividing: light from darkness. The verse displays the order: established. The act displays God approving and ordering the light. The verse displays the light: good. The verse displays the darkness: divided. The verse displays the order: appointed. God seeing the light good and dividing it from darkness displays the ordering of the first work: God saw the light was good and divided it from the darkness — the divine approval of the creation and the first act of separating and ordering, light and darkness assigned their places. The verse displays the divided light, named day and night (v.5).
5:
"And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day." The witnesses note the naming of day and night and the first day — the crux of the creative "day." On "God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night," the grammatical voices note the naming. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God called the light Day, and the darkness... Night" — God names the light and darkness, assigning them their names and appointed alternation; the naming marks God's ordering and dominion over his works; day and night are established.

On "the evening and the morning were the first day," interpreters divide over the length of the creative day. The reformed Calvin and Gill (with the traditional reading) take the "day" as a natural day of twenty-four hours — the evening and morning making one ordinary day; the vicissitude of light and darkness in the space of a day; the six days as six literal days. The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the creative "day" as "not a period of [24] hours, but an æon, or period of indefinite duration" — the day-age reading, the days as long, successive periods of God's creative work. A third view (Augustine, noted by Ellicott) takes the days figuratively, as the order of creation rather than literal time, or even an instantaneous creation framed in days for human understanding.

On the "first day," the eastern Basil notes the unity: Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on "one day" as the measure of time, the day completed by evening and morning; he treats the day reverently as the divinely appointed first measure of created time. The verse displays the naming of day and night and the first day.

The witnesses agree the substance: God named the light Day and the darkness Night, and evening and morning made the first day — the establishment of the day as the measure of created time (Keil, Ellicott, Calvin, Basil). But the length of the creative "day" bears a crux: a literal natural day of twenty-four hours (Calvin, Gill, the traditional reading) vs an æon or period of indefinite duration (Ellicott, the day-age view) vs Augustine's figurative or instantaneous reading. The verse displays the day crux. The verse marks the naming: Day and Night. The verse displays the debate: literal day or age. The verse displays the measure: evening and morning. The verse displays the first day. The vision displays the first day of creation appointed. The verse displays the day: named. The verse displays the night: named. The verse displays the period: first day. The naming of day and night and the first day display the close of the first day and a crux: God named the light Day and the darkness Night, and evening and morning were the first day — read by the traditional view as a literal twenty-four-hour day (Calvin, Gill), by the day-age view as an æon of indefinite length (Ellicott), and by Augustine figuratively. The verse displays the first day, before the firmament of the second (v.6).
6:
"And God said, Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters." The witnesses note the creation of the firmament dividing the waters. On "Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters," the grammatical voices note the second day's work. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Let there be a firmament (raqia, an expanse) in the midst of the waters" — God creates the firmament, the expanse (the sky/atmosphere) spread out in the midst of the primeval waters; the word raqia denotes something stretched or beaten out, the expanse of heaven; God's word brings forth the expanse that orders the waters.

On "a firmament in the midst of the waters," the eastern Basil and the witnesses note the expanse. Basil (Hexaemeron) and Gill note that the firmament is the expanse of the heavens (the atmosphere or the visible sky); not necessarily a solid vault, but the spread-out region that separates the waters; Basil reflects on the nature of this expanse as God's appointed division.

On "let it divide the waters from the waters," the grammatical-historical and reformed Calvin note the separation: "let it divide the waters from the waters" — the firmament separates the waters below (seas, rivers, the waters on the earth) from the waters above (the clouds, the atmospheric waters); God orders the waters by the expanse; the second act of separation in the creation (after light and darkness); the waters are divided and assigned their places. The verse displays the creation of the firmament dividing the waters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the firmament — "Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters," God creating the expanse (raqia) in the midst of the waters (Keil, Ellicott, Basil); the firmament the expanse of the heavens, the spread-out region (Basil, Gill); "let it divide the waters from the waters," the firmament separating the waters below from the waters above (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the firmament. The verse marks the firmament: the expanse. The verse displays the place: midst of the waters. The verse displays the division: waters from waters. The verse displays the order: established. The act displays the expanse dividing the waters. The verse displays the firmament: created. The verse displays the waters: divided. The verse displays the heavens: spread. The creation of the firmament dividing the waters displays the second day's work: God said, "Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters," to divide the waters from the waters — the expanse of heaven spread out to separate and order the waters above and below. The verse displays the dividing firmament, made and named Heaven (vv.7-8).
7:
"And God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament: and it was so." The brief verse notes the making of the firmament and the dividing of the waters. The grammatical voices note the accomplishment. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament" — God carries out the word of v.6, making the expanse and dividing the waters into those below (on and around the earth) and those above (the atmospheric waters/clouds); the command of v.6 is accomplished in the act of v.7.

On "divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above," the witnesses note the ordered waters. Ellicott and Gill note that the waters are divided by the expanse — the waters below (seas, the deep) and the waters above (the clouds, suspended in the heavens); the firmament holds the upper and lower waters apart; the ordering of the waters by God's appointment.

On "and it was so," the grammatical-historical note the efficacy: "and it was so" — the divine word was accomplished; what God commanded (v.6) came to pass; the recurring "it was so" marks the unfailing efficacy of God's creative word; the work is done as God spoke. The verse displays the making of the firmament and the dividing of the waters.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the accomplishment — "God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above," God carrying out the word, dividing the waters below from above (Keil, Ellicott); the waters ordered by the expanse, below and above (Ellicott, Gill); "and it was so," the efficacy of God's word, the work done (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divided waters. The verse marks the firmament: made. The verse displays the waters: below and above. The verse displays the division: accomplished. The verse displays the word: effective. The act displays the firmament dividing the waters as God commanded. The verse displays the firmament: established. The verse displays the waters: ordered. The verse displays the command: fulfilled. The making of the firmament and the dividing of the waters display the accomplishment of the second day's word: God made the firmament and divided the waters under it from the waters above it, and it was so — the expanse established and the waters ordered as God commanded. The verse displays the divided waters, the firmament named Heaven (v.8).
8:
"And God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the morning were the second day." The very brief verse notes the firmament named Heaven and the second day. The grammatical voices note the naming. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God called the firmament Heaven" — God names the expanse "Heaven" (shamayim), as he named the light Day (v.5); the naming marks his ordering and dominion; the firmament/expanse is the visible heaven, the sky.

On "God called the firmament Heaven," the witnesses note the heaven. Gill and Ellicott note that the firmament is called "Heaven" — the visible heavens, the sky above; the expanse that the eye beholds; God gives it its name and place in the ordered creation.

On "the evening and the morning were the second day," the grammatical-historical note the close of the day: "the evening and the morning were the second day" — the second day of creation is completed (the refrain, as v.5); notably, the approval "it was good" is not stated for the second day (the work of dividing the waters being completed only with the gathering of the seas on the third day, v.10); the second day closes with the firmament made. The verse displays the firmament named Heaven and the second day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the naming — "God called the firmament Heaven," God naming the expanse Heaven, as he named the Day (v.5, Keil, Ellicott); the firmament the visible heaven, the sky (Gill, Ellicott); "the evening and the morning were the second day," the close of the second day (the "good" deferred to v.10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named heaven. The verse marks the firmament: named Heaven. The verse displays the naming: God's. The verse displays the day: the second. The verse displays the close: of the day. The act displays the firmament named and the day completed. The verse displays the heaven: named. The verse displays the day: second. The verse displays the creation: ordered. The firmament named Heaven and the second day display the close of the second day's work: God called the firmament Heaven, and evening and morning were the second day — the expanse named and the day completed in the ordering of the creation. The verse displays the named heaven, before the gathering of the seas (v.9).
9:
"And God said, Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place, and let the dry land appear: and it was so." The witnesses note the gathering of the seas and the appearing of dry land. On "Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place," the grammatical voices note the third day's work. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place" — God commands the waters below the firmament to be gathered into one place (the seas), so that the dry land may appear; the ordering and confining of the waters into their bounds; the separation of land and sea.

On "let the dry land appear," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the emerging earth. Henry and Gill note that as the waters are gathered, "the dry land appear[s]" — the earth, before covered by the waters, emerges; the dry land is uncovered and made fit for habitation and vegetation; the separation of land from sea prepares the earth for life.

On "and it was so," the eastern Basil and grammatical-historical note the efficacy: "and it was so" — the command is accomplished; Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on the gathering of the waters and the emergence of the land as the work of God's word, the waters obeying their bounds; God's word orders the sea and land; the dry land appears at his command. The verse displays the gathering of the seas and the appearing of dry land.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gathering — "Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place," God gathering the waters into the seas (Keil, Ellicott); "let the dry land appear," the earth emerging from the waters, made fit for life (Henry, Gill); "and it was so," the command accomplished, the waters obeying their bounds (Basil, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered seas. The verse marks the waters: gathered. The verse displays the land: appearing. The verse displays the separation: sea and land. The verse displays the word: effective. The act displays the waters confined and the dry land uncovered. The verse displays the seas: gathered. The verse displays the dry land: appearing. The verse displays the order: established. The gathering of the seas and the appearing of dry land display the third day's first work: God gathered the waters into one place and the dry land appeared, and it was so — the waters confined to the seas and the earth uncovered, made ready for vegetation. The verse displays the dry land, named Earth and seen good (v.10).
10:
"And God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas: and God saw that it was good." The brief verse notes the naming of earth and seas and God's approval. The grammatical voices note the naming. Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke note that "God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas" — God names the dry land "Earth" and the gathered waters "Seas," as he named the Day and Heaven; the naming marks his ordering and dominion over the land and sea; the elements receive their names and bounds.

On "God called the dry land Earth... the waters... Seas," the witnesses note the order. Keil and Gill note that the naming of Earth and Seas completes the separation of land and water; each is given its name, place, and bounds; the ordered distinction of land and sea by God's appointment.

On "God saw that it was good," the grammatical-historical note the restored refrain: "God saw that it was good" — the approval, omitted on the second day (v.8), now returns, for the separation of the waters is completed with the gathering of the seas; the work of ordering the earth and sea is pronounced good; the creation answers God's design. The verse displays the naming of earth and seas and God's approval.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the naming — "God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas," God naming the land and sea, ordering them (Keil, Clarke); the naming completing the separation of land and water (Keil, Gill); "God saw that it was good," the restored refrain of approval (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named earth. The verse marks the land: Earth. The verse displays the waters: Seas. The verse displays the approval: it was good. The verse displays the order: completed. The act displays the land and sea named and approved. The verse displays the earth: named. The verse displays the seas: named. The verse displays the work: good. The naming of earth and seas and God's approval display the ordering of land and water: God called the dry land Earth and the waters Seas, and saw that it was good — the land and sea named, bounded, and pronounced good. The verse displays the ordered earth, brought to bring forth vegetation (v.11).
11:
"And God said, Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his kind, whose seed is in itself, upon the earth: and it was so." The witnesses note the creation of vegetation, each after its kind. On "Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree," the grammatical voices note the third day's second work. Ellicott and the witnesses note that "Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit" — God commands the earth to produce vegetation: grass, seed-bearing herbs, and fruit trees; the earth, now uncovered and ordered, is made fruitful at God's word; the first living things, the plants, are brought forth.

On "after his kind, whose seed is in itself," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reproduction after kind. Calvin and Ellicott note that the plants are made "after his kind, whose seed is in itself" — each species bears seed to reproduce after its own kind; the principle of reproduction according to kind is established at creation; the plants are endowed with seed to perpetuate their species; the order and fixity of the kinds.

On "let the earth bring forth," the eastern Basil notes the earth's fruitfulness: Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on the word "let the earth bring forth" — the earth, at God's command, brings forth vegetation in marvelous variety and order; the divine word endues the earth with fruitfulness; the once-barren land teems with plants, each after its kind; the wisdom and bounty of God displayed in the green world. The verse displays the creation of vegetation, each after its kind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vegetation — "Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree," God making the earth fruitful with plants (Ellicott); "after his kind, whose seed is in itself," reproduction according to kind, the seed perpetuating the species (Calvin, Ellicott); "let the earth bring forth," the earth endued with fruitfulness at God's word (Basil). The verse displays the fruitful earth. The verse marks the command: bring forth grass. The verse displays the herbs: seed-bearing. The verse displays the trees: fruit-bearing. The verse displays the kinds: after their kind. The act displays the earth made fruitful with vegetation. The verse displays the grass: brought forth. The verse displays the seed: in itself. The verse displays the kinds: fixed. The creation of vegetation, each after its kind, displays the third day's second work: God commanded the earth to bring forth grass, seed-bearing herbs, and fruit trees after their kind, and it was so — the earth made fruitful with plants endowed to reproduce after their kinds. The verse displays the fruitful earth, bringing forth as commanded (v.12).
12:
"And the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit, whose seed was in itself, after his kind: and God saw that it was good." The brief verse notes the earth bringing forth vegetation as commanded. The grammatical voices note the accomplishment. Clarke and Gill note that "the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit... after his kind" — the earth, at God's word (v.11), brings forth the vegetation; the command is fulfilled; the grass, herbs, and fruit trees appear, each after its kind, with seed to reproduce; the word of God accomplished in the fruitful earth.

On "after his kind," the witnesses note the fixity of kinds. Clarke and Gill note the repeated "after his kind" — each plant brings forth after its own kind, with its seed in itself; the species are distinct and perpetuate themselves; the order of the kinds, established at creation, is maintained in reproduction.

On "God saw that it was good," the grammatical-historical note the approval: "God saw that it was good" — the vegetation, the work of the third day, is pronounced good; the fruitful earth answers God's design; the second "good" of the third day (with v.10) marks the completeness and fitness of the earth and its plants. The verse displays the earth bringing forth vegetation as commanded.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the accomplishment — "the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit," the command fulfilled, the earth bringing forth the vegetation (Clarke, Gill); the repeated "after his kind," the fixity and perpetuation of the kinds (Clarke, Gill); "God saw that it was good," the approval of the fruitful earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruitful earth. The verse marks the grass: brought forth. The verse displays the herbs: after their kind. The verse displays the trees: fruit-bearing. The verse displays the approval: it was good. The act displays the earth fruitful as God commanded. The verse displays the vegetation: produced. The verse displays the kinds: maintained. The verse displays the work: good. The earth bringing forth vegetation as commanded displays the fulfillment of the third day's word: the earth brought forth grass, herbs, and fruit trees after their kind, and God saw that it was good — the fruitful earth answering God's command and pronounced good. The verse displays the green earth, closing the third day (v.13).
13:
"And the evening and the morning were the third day." The very brief verse notes the close of the third day. The witnesses note the completed day. Gill notes that "the evening and the morning were the third day" — the third day of creation is completed (the refrain, as vv.5, 8); on this day God gathered the seas, uncovered the dry land, and made it bring forth vegetation; the third day's twofold work (the separation of land and sea, and the clothing of the earth with plants) is finished.

On "the evening and the morning," the witnesses note the measure of the day. Gill notes that "the evening and the morning" mark the bounds of the day (as in the previous days); the day completed by its evening and morning; the third in the succession of the creative days.

On "the third day," the grammatical-historical note the place in the order: the third day is the close of the first triad of days (light, firmament, land and vegetation) — the forming and ordering of the realms (heaven, sea, and land), which the second triad (days four to six) will fill with their inhabitants (lights, fish and fowl, beasts and man); the third day completes the preparation of the earth as the seat of life. The verse displays the close of the third day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the close — "the evening and the morning were the third day," the third creative day completed, its twofold work finished (Gill); "the evening and the morning" the bounds of the day (Gill); the third day closing the first triad of forming days (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the third day. The verse marks the evening: and morning. The verse displays the day: the third. The verse displays the work: completed. The verse displays the order: the forming days. The close displays the third day finished in the order of creation. The verse displays the evening: come. The verse displays the morning: come. The verse displays the day: third. The close of the third day displays the completion of the earth's forming: evening and morning were the third day — the seas gathered, the land uncovered, and the earth clothed with vegetation, the third day finishing the preparation of the world for life. The verse displays the finished third day, before the lights of the fourth (v.14).
14:
"And God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years:" The witnesses note the creation of the heavenly lights for signs and seasons. On "Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night," the grammatical voices note the fourth day's work. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Let there be lights (luminaries) in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night" — God appoints the heavenly lights (sun, moon, stars) in the firmament to divide day from night; the lights are set to rule and measure the times; the fourth day fills the heavens with their luminaries.

On "let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years," the witnesses note their offices. Ellicott and Gill note that the lights serve "for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years" — they mark times: signs (of weather, of God's appointments, of seasons), seasons (the fixed times, festivals, agricultural seasons), days and years (the measures of time); the heavenly bodies are God's appointed timekeepers for the earth.

On the relation to the light of day one, the eastern Basil and the reformed Calvin note the order: Basil (Hexaemeron) and Calvin reflect that the light created on the first day is now gathered into and ordered by the luminaries; the sun and moon are not the source of light's existence but its appointed vessels and rulers; God made the light before the lights, that we might know light depends on him, not on the sun; the lights are ministers of the divine appointment. The verse displays the creation of the heavenly lights for signs and seasons.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lights — "Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night," God appointing the luminaries to divide day and night (Keil, Ellicott); "for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years," the lights as God's timekeepers (Ellicott, Gill); the light of day one now ordered into the luminaries, light from God not the sun (Basil, Calvin). The verse displays the heavenly lights. The verse marks the lights: in the firmament. The verse displays the office: divide day and night. The verse displays the use: signs and seasons. The verse displays the measure: days and years. The act displays the luminaries appointed to rule and measure time. The verse displays the lights: created. The verse displays the seasons: marked. The verse displays the times: ordered. The creation of the heavenly lights for signs and seasons displays the fourth day's work: God set lights in the firmament to divide day from night and to serve for signs, seasons, days, and years — the luminaries appointed as God's timekeepers, the light of day one now ordered into its vessels. The verse displays the heavenly lights, giving light upon the earth (v.15).
15:
"And let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth: and it was so." The brief verse notes the lights set to give light upon the earth. The grammatical voices note their purpose. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth" — beyond dividing day and night and marking times (v.14), the lights serve to give light upon the earth; their primary office toward the earth is illumination; they dispense light to the world for the benefit of its creatures.

On "to give light upon the earth," the witnesses note the illumination. Calvin and Ellicott note that the lights "give light upon the earth" — the sun, moon, and stars illumine the earth, providing the light by which life and labor proceed; the heavens serve the earth; the lights are appointed for the earth's good.

On "and it was so," the grammatical-historical note the efficacy: "and it was so" — the command is accomplished; the lights are set to give light as God appointed; the recurring "it was so" marks the efficacy of God's word; the luminaries fulfill their office at his command. The verse displays the lights set to give light upon the earth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose — "let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth," the lights serving to illumine the earth (Keil, Ellicott); "to give light upon the earth," the heavens serving the earth with light (Calvin, Ellicott); "and it was so," the efficacy of God's word (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the illumining lights. The verse marks the lights: in the firmament. The verse displays the office: give light. The verse displays the beneficiary: the earth. The verse displays the word: effective. The act displays the lights set to illumine the earth. The verse displays the lights: appointed. The verse displays the earth: illumined. The verse displays the command: fulfilled. The lights set to give light upon the earth display the office of the luminaries: God appointed them to give light upon the earth, and it was so — the heavens serving the earth with light, the lights fulfilling their office at God's word. The verse displays the illumining lights, made and set in the heavens (v.16).
16:
"And God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night: he made the stars also." The witnesses note the making of the sun, moon, and stars. On "God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night," the grammatical voices note the sun and moon. Clarke and Ellicott note that "God made two great lights; the greater light (the sun) to rule the day, and the lesser light (the moon) to rule the night" — God appoints the two great lights, the sun to govern the day and the moon the night; "great" not in absolute size but in appearance and office toward the earth; the sun and moon are the chief luminaries, set to rule the times.

On "the greater light... the lesser light," the reformed Calvin notes the accommodation. Calvin notes that the sun and moon are called the "two great lights" as they appear to us and serve us — Moses speaks according to common perception, not astronomical exactness; the moon, though smaller than many stars, is "great" in its nearness and office to the earth; God describes his works as they serve human life.

On "he made the stars also," the eastern Basil and grammatical-historical note the stars: "he made the stars also" — the countless stars are made by God, added almost as in passing, yet displaying the vastness of his work; Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on the multitude and order of the stars as the work of God, set in the heavens to adorn them and to serve; the sun, moon, and stars together are God's creatures, not deities; against the worship of the heavenly bodies. The verse displays the making of the sun, moon, and stars.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lights — "God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night," the sun and moon to govern day and night (Clarke, Ellicott); the lights "great" in appearance and office, Moses speaking by common perception (Calvin); "he made the stars also," the stars made by God, adorning the heavens (Basil, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly bodies. The verse marks the great lights: two. The verse displays the sun: ruling day. The verse displays the moon: ruling night. The verse displays the stars: made also. The act displays the sun, moon, and stars made by God. The verse displays the sun: greater. The verse displays the moon: lesser. The verse displays the stars: created. The making of the sun, moon, and stars displays the fourth day's furnishing of the heavens: God made the two great lights to rule day and night, and the stars also — the luminaries, God's creatures, set to govern the times and adorn the heavens, not deities but servants of his order. The verse displays the heavenly lights, set in the firmament (v.17).
17:
"And God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth," The very brief verse notes God setting the lights in the heavens to illumine the earth. The witnesses note the placing of the lights. Gill notes that "God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth" — God places the sun, moon, and stars in the expanse of the heaven, appointing them their stations, that they may give light upon the earth; the lights are fixed in their places by God's hand and ordained to serve the earth with light.

On "set them in the firmament of the heaven," the witnesses note their stations. Gill notes that God "set them" in the firmament — gave them their places and courses in the heavens; the lights are not wandering by chance but established by God in their appointed stations; the order of the heavens is God's arrangement.

On "to give light upon the earth," the grammatical-historical note the purpose: the lights are set "to give light upon the earth" — the repeated purpose (vv.15-17) emphasizes that the heavens serve the earth; the lights are appointed for the earth's illumination; the heavenly bodies minister to the world below. The verse displays God setting the lights in the heavens to illumine the earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the placing — "God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth," God placing the lights in their stations to illumine the earth (Gill); "set them in the firmament," the lights established in their appointed places (Gill); "to give light upon the earth," the heavens serving the earth with light (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the set lights. The verse marks the setting: in the firmament. The verse displays the stations: appointed. The verse displays the purpose: give light. The verse displays the earth: illumined. The act displays the lights established to serve the earth. The verse displays the lights: set. The verse displays the heavens: ordered. The verse displays the earth: served. God setting the lights in the heavens to illumine the earth displays the establishing of the luminaries: God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth — the sun, moon, and stars fixed in their stations to serve the earth with light. The verse displays the set lights, ruling day and night (v.18).
18:
"And to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness: and God saw that it was good." The very brief verse notes the lights ruling day and night and the divine approval. The witnesses note the rule of the lights. Gill notes that the lights are set "to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness" — the sun governs the day and the moon (and stars) the night; the lights rule and order the times, dividing light from darkness; the heavenly bodies are God's appointed governors of day and night.

On "to rule over the day and over the night," the witnesses note the dominion of the lights. Gill notes that the sun and moon "rule" day and night — not as conscious rulers but as appointed governors of the times; they preside over and measure the day and night by God's ordinance; the order of the times is in their keeping.

On "God saw that it was good," the grammatical-historical note the approval: "God saw that it was good" — the work of the fourth day, the lights, is pronounced good; the heavens furnished with their luminaries answer God's design; the refrain of approval marks the fitness and beauty of the heavenly order. The verse displays the lights ruling day and night and the divine approval.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rule — "to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness," the lights governing and ordering the times (Gill); "to rule over the day and over the night," the sun and moon as appointed governors of the times (Gill); "God saw that it was good," the approval of the fourth day's work (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ruling lights. The verse marks the rule: day and night. The verse displays the dividing: light from darkness. The verse displays the approval: it was good. The verse displays the order: established. The act displays the lights ruling the times, approved by God. The verse displays the lights: ruling. The verse displays the times: ordered. The verse displays the work: good. The lights ruling day and night and the divine approval display the close of the fourth day's furnishing: the lights rule day and night and divide light from darkness, and God saw that it was good — the heavenly luminaries appointed to govern the times, the heavens pronounced good. The verse displays the ruling lights, closing the fourth day (v.19).
19:
"And the evening and the morning were the fourth day." The very brief verse notes the close of the fourth day. The witnesses note the completed day. Gill notes that "the evening and the morning were the fourth day" — the fourth day of creation is completed (the refrain, as vv.5, 8, 13); on this day God furnished the heavens with the sun, moon, and stars, appointing them to divide and rule the times; the fourth day's work is finished.

On "the evening and the morning," the witnesses note the measure. Gill notes that "the evening and the morning" mark the bounds of the day; the day completed by its evening and morning; the fourth in the succession of the creative days.

On "the fourth day," the grammatical-historical note the place in the order: the fourth day opens the second triad of days (lights, fish and fowl, beasts and man) — the filling of the realms formed in the first triad; as the first day made light and the fourth made the lights, the days correspond; the fourth day begins the furnishing of the heavens and the times with their rulers. The verse displays the close of the fourth day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the close — "the evening and the morning were the fourth day," the fourth creative day completed, the heavens furnished with their lights (Gill); "the evening and the morning" the bounds of the day (Gill); the fourth day opening the second triad of filling days, corresponding to the first (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fourth day. The verse marks the evening: and morning. The verse displays the day: the fourth. The verse displays the work: completed. The verse displays the order: the filling days. The close displays the fourth day finished in the order of creation. The verse displays the evening: come. The verse displays the morning: come. The verse displays the day: fourth. The close of the fourth day displays the furnishing of the heavens: evening and morning were the fourth day — the sun, moon, and stars made and set to rule the times, the fourth day finishing the furnishing of the heavens. The verse displays the finished fourth day, before the fish and fowl of the fifth (v.20).
20:
"And God said, Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven." The witnesses note the creation of fish and fowl from the waters. On "Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life," the grammatical voices note the fifth day's work. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life" — God commands the waters to teem with living creatures; the first animal life, the swarming creatures of the sea; the waters bring forth abundantly at God's word; the seas are filled with their inhabitants.

On "the moving creature that hath life," the witnesses note the living creatures. Calvin and Gill note that "the moving creature that hath life (a living soul, nephesh)" — the sea-creatures are endowed with animal life, a living principle beyond the plants; the first creatures bearing the "living soul"; the abundance and variety of sea-life brought forth.

On "fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven," the eastern Basil and the witnesses note the birds: God also makes "fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven" — the birds, made to fly in the open expanse; Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on the marvelous variety of the winged creatures and the fish, the bounty and wisdom of God filling the waters and the air with life; the fifth day fills the sea and sky with their living inhabitants. The verse displays the creation of fish and fowl from the waters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creatures — "Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life," God filling the waters with swarming life (Keil, Ellicott); "the moving creature that hath life," the sea-creatures endowed with animal life (Calvin, Gill); "fowl that may fly... in the open firmament," the birds made to fly in the expanse (Basil). The verse displays the living creatures. The verse marks the waters: teeming. The verse displays the creatures: living. The verse displays the fowl: flying. The verse displays the firmament: open. The act displays the sea and sky filled with life. The verse displays the waters: swarming. The verse displays the fish: brought forth. The verse displays the birds: flying. The creation of fish and fowl from the waters displays the fifth day's work: God commanded the waters to bring forth abundantly the living creatures, and the birds to fly in the open firmament — the seas and the sky filled with their living inhabitants by God's word. The verse displays the teeming waters, the great creatures created (v.21).
21:
"And God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good." The witnesses note the creation of the great sea-creatures and birds after their kind. On "God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth," the grammatical voices note the divine creation. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God created (bara) great whales (great sea-creatures, tanninim), and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly" — the word "created" (bara, as in v.1) is used again, marking the bringing forth of animal life as a fresh, distinctive creative act; the great sea-creatures and all the swarming life of the waters are God's creation.

On "great whales," the witnesses note the great creatures. Calvin and Gill note that "great whales (tanninim)" — the great sea-monsters or sea-creatures; the largest of the creatures specially mentioned, displaying the power of God; the great and small alike are his work.

On "after their kind... and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good," the grammatical-historical note the kinds and approval: the creatures are made "after their kind," each species distinct and reproducing after its kind; and "God saw that it was good" — the fifth day's work, the sea-creatures and birds, is pronounced good; the living world answers God's design. The verse displays the creation of the great sea-creatures and birds after their kind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creation — "God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth," the word bara marking the creation of animal life (Keil, Ellicott); "great whales (tanninim)," the great sea-creatures displaying God's power (Calvin, Gill); "after their kind... and God saw that it was good," the kinds distinct, the work approved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the created sea-life. The verse marks the creation: bara. The verse displays the whales: great. The verse displays the kinds: after their kind. The verse displays the approval: it was good. The act displays the great and small sea-creatures created by God. The verse displays the whales: created. The verse displays the fowl: after its kind. The verse displays the work: good. The creation of the great sea-creatures and birds after their kind displays the furnishing of sea and sky: God created the great sea-creatures and all the swarming life of the waters, and the birds, after their kinds, and saw that it was good — the living world of sea and air, the work of God's creative word, pronounced good. The verse displays the living creatures, blessed to multiply (v.22).
22:
"And God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth." The brief verse notes God blessing the creatures to multiply. The grammatical voices note the blessing. Clarke and Ellicott note that "God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth" — God blesses the sea-creatures and birds, endowing them with the power and command to be fruitful, multiply, and fill the seas and the earth; the first blessing in Scripture, the gift of fruitfulness and increase to the living creatures.

On "Be fruitful, and multiply," the witnesses note the gift of increase. Calvin and Gill note that "be fruitful, and multiply" is both a blessing and an ordinance — God grants and commands the creatures to propagate their kinds; the power of reproduction and increase is a divine gift; the creatures fill their realms by God's blessing.

On "fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth," the grammatical-historical note the filling of the realms: the creatures are to "fill the waters in the seas" and the birds to "multiply in the earth" — the sea and sky are to be filled with their inhabitants; God's blessing ensures the increase and abundance of life; the realms formed in the first triad are filled in the second. The verse displays God blessing the creatures to multiply.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply," God blessing the creatures with fruitfulness, the first blessing (Clarke, Ellicott); "be fruitful, and multiply," the gift and ordinance of increase (Calvin, Gill); "fill the waters... and let fowl multiply," the filling of sea and sky (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed creatures. The verse marks the blessing: God blessed them. The verse displays the command: be fruitful. The verse displays the increase: multiply. The verse displays the filling: seas and earth. The blessing displays the creatures endowed to increase and fill their realms. The verse displays the creatures: blessed. The verse displays the fruitfulness: granted. The verse displays the realms: filled. God blessing the creatures to multiply displays the first blessing of Scripture: God blessed the sea-creatures and birds, bidding them be fruitful, multiply, and fill the seas and the earth — the gift of fruitfulness and increase to the living world. The verse displays the blessed creatures, closing the fifth day (v.23).
23:
"And the evening and the morning were the fifth day." The very brief verse notes the close of the fifth day. The witnesses note the completed day. Gill and Ellicott note that "the evening and the morning were the fifth day" — the fifth day of creation is completed (the refrain, as the previous days); on this day God filled the waters with sea-creatures and the air with birds, and blessed them to multiply; the fifth day's work is finished.

On "the evening and the morning," the witnesses note the measure. Gill notes that "the evening and the morning" mark the bounds of the day; the day completed by its evening and morning; the fifth in the succession of the creative days.

On "the fifth day," the grammatical-historical note the place in the order: the fifth day, the second of the filling triad, corresponds to the second day (the firmament dividing the waters) — as the second day made the sea and sky, the fifth fills them with fish and fowl; the second triad fills the realms of the first; the fifth day furnishes the waters and the air with their living inhabitants. The verse displays the close of the fifth day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the close — "the evening and the morning were the fifth day," the fifth creative day completed, the sea and air filled with life (Gill, Ellicott); "the evening and the morning" the bounds of the day (Gill); the fifth day filling the realms of the second, corresponding to it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fifth day. The verse marks the evening: and morning. The verse displays the day: the fifth. The verse displays the work: completed. The verse displays the order: the filling days. The close displays the fifth day finished in the order of creation. The verse displays the evening: come. The verse displays the morning: come. The verse displays the day: fifth. The close of the fifth day displays the furnishing of sea and air: evening and morning were the fifth day — the waters filled with sea-creatures and the air with birds, the fifth day finishing the furnishing of the sea and sky. The verse displays the finished fifth day, before the land animals and man of the sixth (v.24).
24:
"And God said, Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth after his kind: and it was so." The witnesses note the creation of the land animals after their kind. On "Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind," the grammatical voices note the sixth day's first work. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind" — God commands the earth to bring forth the land animals; the living creatures of the dry land, after their kinds; the sixth day begins with the furnishing of the earth with its animal inhabitants.

On "cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth," the witnesses note the threefold classes. Calvin and Gill note the classes: "cattle (the domesticable animals), and creeping thing (the smaller creatures, reptiles, insects), and beast of the earth (the wild beasts)" — the land animals in their kinds, tame and wild, great and small; the earth furnished with its varied animal life.

On "after his kind... and it was so," the eastern Basil and grammatical-historical note the kinds and efficacy: the animals are made "after his kind," each species distinct and reproducing after its kind; Basil (Hexaemeron) reflects on the variety, instincts, and order of the land animals as the work of God's wisdom; "and it was so" — the command accomplished, the earth bringing forth its creatures at God's word. The verse displays the creation of the land animals after their kind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creatures — "Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind," God making the earth bring forth the land animals (Keil, Ellicott); "cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth," the threefold classes of land animals (Calvin, Gill); "after his kind... and it was so," the kinds distinct, the command accomplished (Basil, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the land animals. The verse marks the command: bring forth. The verse displays the classes: cattle, creeping, beasts. The verse displays the kinds: after their kind. The verse displays the word: effective. The act displays the earth bringing forth its animal life. The verse displays the creatures: brought forth. The verse displays the kinds: distinct. The verse displays the earth: furnished. The creation of the land animals after their kind displays the sixth day's first work: God commanded the earth to bring forth the living creatures — cattle, creeping things, and beasts — after their kinds, and it was so; the earth furnished with its varied animal life by God's word. The verse displays the land animals, made and seen good (v.25).
25:
"And God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind: and God saw that it was good." The brief verse notes the making of the land animals and the divine approval. The grammatical voices note the accomplishment. Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke note that "God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind" — God carries out the word of v.24, making the land animals in their three classes (wild beasts, cattle, creeping things), each after its kind; the command is accomplished in the act; the earth furnished with its animal inhabitants.

On "after his kind," the witnesses note the fixity of kinds. Clarke and Gill note the repeated "after his kind" — each animal made and reproducing after its own kind; the species distinct and perpetuating themselves; the order of the kinds, established at creation, maintained among the animals.

On "God saw that it was good," the eastern Basil and grammatical-historical note the approval: "God saw that it was good" — the land animals, the sixth day's first work, are pronounced good; Basil reflects on the goodness and fitness of each creature in its kind; the animal creation answers God's design and is approved by him; the work is good before man is made. The verse displays the making of the land animals and the divine approval.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the accomplishment — "God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth," God making the land animals in their kinds (Keil, Clarke); the repeated "after his kind," the fixity and perpetuation of the kinds (Clarke, Gill); "God saw that it was good," the approval of the animal creation (Basil, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the made animals. The verse marks the beasts: made. The verse displays the cattle: and creeping things. The verse displays the kinds: after their kind. The verse displays the approval: it was good. The act displays the land animals made and approved. The verse displays the animals: created. The verse displays the kinds: maintained. The verse displays the work: good. The making of the land animals and the divine approval display the accomplishment of the sixth day's first work: God made the beasts, cattle, and creeping things after their kinds, and saw that it was good — the animal creation answering God's command and pronounced good before the making of man. The verse displays the good animals, before the making of man (v.26).
26:
"And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth." The witnesses note the making of man in God's image — the crux of "let us make man." On "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness," interpreters divide over the plural "us." The reformed Gill and Calvin, the puritan Henry, and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke (the Christian tradition) take "Let us make man" as the language of the plurality of persons in the Godhead — God the Father speaking to the Son and the Holy Spirit; the consultation within the Trinity over the making of man (Henry: "the three persons"); the plural marks the divine counsel of the Triune God. A second reading (Jewish, e.g. Saadiah, noted by Gill) takes the plural as the plural of majesty or honour (God speaking as kings do). A third reading (the Targum of Jonathan, noted and rejected by Gill) takes God as addressing the angels — which Gill rejects, since man was not made in the angels' image nor by their power.

On "man in our image, after our likeness," the witnesses note the image of God. Henry, Gill, and Calvin note that man is made "in the image of God" — the image consists chiefly in the rational, immortal soul, and in the moral likeness of knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness (Ephesians 4:24; Colossians 3:10); the soul especially bears God's image; man is made godlike in nature and (originally) in holiness.

On "let them have dominion," the grammatical-historical and reformed note the dominion: man is given "dominion over the fish... fowl... cattle... and all the earth" — the rule over the creatures, part of the image (man as God's vicegerent over the earth); the dominion belongs to man as bearing God's image; "them" (male and female, v.27) shows the dominion given to mankind. The verse displays the making of man in God's image.

The witnesses agree the substance: God resolves to make man in his image and likeness, to have dominion over the creatures (Gill, Calvin, Henry, Clarke). But the plural "let us make" bears a crux: the plurality of persons in the Godhead, the Triune God's counsel (the Christian reading — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke) vs the plural of majesty (Jewish, Saadiah) vs God addressing the angels (the Targum, rejected by Gill). The verse displays the "let us make man" crux. The verse marks the counsel: let us make man. The verse displays the debate: Trinity, majesty, or angels. The verse displays the image: of God. The verse displays the dominion: over the creatures. The resolve displays the Triune God making man in his image to rule the earth. The verse displays the man: in God's image. The verse displays the likeness: God's. The verse displays the dominion: granted. The making of man in God's image displays the crown of creation and a crux: God said, "Let us make man in our image" — read by the Christian tradition as the counsel of the persons of the Godhead (Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke), by the Jewish as the plural of majesty, and (rejected) as God addressing the angels; man made in God's image to have dominion over the creatures. The verse displays the imaged man, created male and female (v.27).
27:
"So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them." The witnesses note the creation of man, male and female, in God's image. On "So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him," the grammatical voices note the accomplishment. Ellicott and Gill note that "God created (bara) man in his own image, in the image of God created he him" — God carries out the resolve of v.26, creating man in his own image; the threefold repetition of "created" (bara) and "image" emphasizes the dignity and distinctiveness of man's creation; man is the crown of the creation, made godlike, the only creature bearing the divine image.

On "in the image of God created he him," the reformed Calvin notes the image. Calvin and Gill note that man is made "in the image of God" — the image (the rational soul, the moral likeness of knowledge, righteousness, and holiness, and the dominion) is the special dignity of man; the emphatic repetition stresses that man, and man alone, bears God's image; the foundation of human dignity and worth.

On "male and female created he them," the grammatical-historical note the two sexes: "male and female created he them" — God created mankind as male and female; both sexes bear the image of God; the distinction of male and female is God's creation, the basis of marriage and the propagation of the race (v.28); humanity in its two sexes is God's handiwork. The verse displays the creation of man, male and female, in God's image.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creation — "God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him," God creating man in his image, the crown of creation (Ellicott, Gill); "in the image of God," the rational soul and moral likeness, man's special dignity (Calvin, Gill); "male and female created he them," both sexes God's creation, bearing his image (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the imaged man. The verse marks the creation: in God's image. The verse displays the dignity: godlike. The verse displays the sexes: male and female. The verse displays the image: borne by man. The act displays man created male and female in God's image. The verse displays the man: imaged. The verse displays the image: of God. The verse displays the sexes: created. The creation of man, male and female, in God's image displays the crown of the creation: so God created man in his own image, male and female — humanity, the only creature bearing the divine image, made male and female by God, the dignity of man established. The verse displays the imaged man and woman, blessed and given dominion (v.28).
28:
"And God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth." The witnesses note the blessing of man with fruitfulness and dominion. On "God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Clarke and Gill note that "God blessed them, and... said, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish (fill) the earth" — God blesses man (as he blessed the creatures, v.22), endowing him with the power and command to multiply and fill the earth; the gift of fruitfulness and the ordinance of marriage and family; mankind is to people the earth.

On "and subdue it," the witnesses note the mandate. Calvin and Gill note that man is to "subdue (subjugate) it" — to bring the earth under cultivation and use; the mandate to till, develop, and master the earth for human good; man is set over the earth to govern and use it; the cultural mandate.

On "have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dominion: man is given "dominion over the fish... fowl... and every living thing" — the rule over all the creatures, the prerogative of the image of God (v.26); man is constituted God's vicegerent over the earth and its creatures; the dominion is a trust under God, the creatures given for man's service and care. The verse displays the blessing of man with fruitfulness and dominion.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "God blessed them... Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it," God blessing man with fruitfulness and the mandate to fill and subdue the earth (Clarke, Gill, Calvin); "subdue it," the mandate to cultivate and master the earth (Calvin, Gill); "have dominion over the fish... and every living thing," the rule over the creatures, the prerogative of the image (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed man. The verse marks the blessing: be fruitful. The verse displays the mandate: replenish and subdue. The verse displays the dominion: over the creatures. The verse displays the trust: under God. The blessing displays man endowed to fill, subdue, and rule the earth. The verse displays the man: blessed. The verse displays the earth: subdued. The verse displays the creatures: ruled. The blessing of man with fruitfulness and dominion displays the commission of mankind: God blessed them, bidding them be fruitful, multiply, fill and subdue the earth, and have dominion over the creatures — the gift of increase and the rule over the earth, man set as God's vicegerent. The verse displays the commissioned man, given the plants for food (v.29).
29:
"And God said, Behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed, which is upon the face of all the earth, and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it shall be for meat." The witnesses note God's gift of the plants for food. On "Behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed... and every tree... yielding seed," the grammatical voices note the provision. Clarke and Ellicott note that "I have given you every herb bearing seed... and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed" — God grants man the seed-bearing herbs and the fruit trees for food; the original provision for mankind is the plants — herbs and fruits; God's bounty supplies man's need from the produce of the earth.

On "to you it shall be for meat," the witnesses note the food. Gill and Henry note that "to you it shall be for meat (food)" — the herbs and fruits are appointed as man's food; in the original creation, the diet given to man is vegetable (the eating of flesh being granted later, after the flood, 9:3); God provides for man's sustenance from the fruitful earth.

On the gift, the puritan Henry notes God's provision: "Behold, I have given you" — God, having made man lord of the earth, provides for his maintenance; the gift of the herbs and fruits is the token of God's fatherly care; man is to live by God's bounty, dependent on the Creator who supplies his food. The verse displays God's gift of the plants for food.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "I have given you every herb bearing seed... and every tree... yielding seed," God granting man the herbs and fruits for food (Clarke, Ellicott); "to you it shall be for meat," the vegetable diet of the original creation (flesh granted later, 9:3, Gill, Henry); "Behold, I have given you," God's fatherly provision for man (Henry). The verse displays the gift of food. The verse marks the gift: every herb and tree. The verse displays the provision: for food. The verse displays the diet: vegetable. The verse displays the care: God's bounty. The gift displays God providing man's food from the earth. The verse displays the herbs: given. The verse displays the fruits: for meat. The verse displays the provision: God's. God's gift of the plants for food displays the Creator's provision for man: God gave man every seed-bearing herb and fruit tree for food — the bounty of the earth supplying man's sustenance, the original vegetable diet, the token of God's fatherly care. The verse displays the provided food, given also to the animals (v.30).
30:
"And to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, and to every thing that creepeth upon the earth, wherein there is life, I have given every green herb for meat: and it was so." The very brief verse notes God's gift of the green herbs to the animals for food. The witnesses note the provision for the creatures. Gill notes that "to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, and to every thing that creepeth... wherein there is life, I have given every green herb for meat" — God provides for the animals as for man; the green herbs (the grasses and plants) are given to the beasts, birds, and creeping things for their food; God's bounty supplies the food of all the living creatures.

On "wherein there is life," the witnesses note the living creatures. Gill notes that the provision is for "every thing... wherein there is life (a living soul)" — all the creatures that have animal life; God cares for the maintenance of all his living creatures, providing their food from the earth.

On "I have given every green herb for meat: and it was so," the grammatical-historical note the original provision: the animals are given "every green herb for meat" — in the original creation, the food of the animals (as of man) is the produce of the earth; God's providence supplies the food of all; "and it was so" — the provision is established as God appointed; God feeds all his creatures. The verse displays God's gift of the green herbs to the animals for food.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "to every beast... and to every fowl... and to every thing that creepeth... I have given every green herb for meat," God providing the green herbs for the animals' food (Gill); "wherein there is life," the provision for all the living creatures (Gill); "every green herb for meat: and it was so," the original food of the animals from the earth, established by God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fed creatures. The verse marks the gift: the green herb. The verse displays the creatures: beasts, birds, creeping things. The verse displays the food: provided. The verse displays the providence: God's. The gift displays God feeding all his living creatures. The verse displays the herbs: given. The verse displays the animals: fed. The verse displays the provision: established. God's gift of the green herbs to the animals for food displays the Creator's care for all the living: God gave every green herb to the beasts, birds, and creeping things for food, and it was so — God's providence supplying the food of all his creatures from the earth. The verse displays the provided creatures, before God's final survey (v.31).
31:
"And God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day." The witnesses note God's survey of all his work as very good and the close of the sixth day. On "God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good," the grammatical voices note the final approval. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good" — God surveys the whole completed creation and pronounces it "very good"; the individual works were each "good" (vv.4, 10, etc.), but the whole, complete and harmonious, with man at its crown, is "very good"; the perfection and goodness of the finished creation.

On "behold, it was very good," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the heightened approval. Calvin and Henry note that the whole creation is "very good" — perfect in its kind, complete, beautiful, and answering God's design; the addition of "very" marks the completeness and harmony of the whole; God's approval of his finished work; the creation, as it came from God's hand, was wholly good, without flaw or evil.

On "the evening and the morning were the sixth day," the grammatical-historical note the close: "the evening and the morning were the sixth day" — the sixth day, the climax of the creative week, is completed; on it God made the land animals and man, the crown of creation; the work of creation is finished, and the seventh day (the rest) follows; the sixth day completes the six days of God's creative work. The verse displays God's survey of all his work as very good and the close of the sixth day.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the final approval — "God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good," God surveying the whole creation and pronouncing it very good (Keil, Ellicott); the whole "very good," perfect and complete, without flaw (Calvin, Henry); "the evening and the morning were the sixth day," the close of the sixth day, the creation finished (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the very good creation. The verse marks the survey: everything made. The verse displays the verdict: very good. The verse displays the whole: complete. The verse displays the day: the sixth. The survey displays the finished creation pronounced very good. The verse displays the works: surveyed. The verse displays the creation: very good. The verse displays the day: sixth. God's survey of all his work as very good and the close of the sixth day display the consummation of creation: God saw everything he had made, and it was very good, and evening and morning were the sixth day — the whole creation, complete and crowned with man, pronounced very good, the six days of God's creative work finished. The verse closes the creation account, before the rest of the seventh day (ch.2).
